aesthetic: The Burnt Manuscript — a conflagration set in the type of an 1837 Romantic folio
anchor: The 1837 first edition (James Fraser, 3 vols. — 'The Bastille / The Constitution / The Guillotine') read against the two facts that organize the whole corpus: the only fair copy of Volume I burned to kindling at Harriet Taylor's hearth on 6 March 1835, and the book's own controlling fire-imagery — Sansculottism as 'the Death-Birth of a World', the 'huge smoke-cloud, streaked with rays as of heaven on one side; girt on the other as with hellfire', and the recurring World-Phoenix of immolation-and-Palingenesia carried over from Sartor. Carlyle told Jane the rewrite came 'direct and flamingly from the heart of a living man' — a draft that had literally been used to make flame.
rationale: The book IS a fire: a providential conflagration in which an ancien regime hollowed to Sham burns and a new world is born of the ash. So the world is incandescent, not cool — ember-crimson and flame-orange against charred ash-paper, with the 'rays as of heaven' in worn gilt and one band of smoke-blue for the doubled valence (heaven on one side, hellfire on the other). The Baskerville-led transitional serif is the 1830s English book face — Romantic, high-rhetorical, declamatory — set apart from the cold Didone of the Friedrich corpus and the Scotch-Roman of the Calvinist corpus. Warm, urgent, apocalyptic — the jeremiad, not the lecture.

fonts.body: Palatino, 'Palatino Linotype', 'Book Antiqua', Georgia, 'Times New Roman', serif
